content: Just as language learners build long-term writing proficiency through iterative, explicit reflection taught in high English curricula (the educational definition of reflexion), large language models can improve their general problem-solving accuracy by formalizing reflexion as a graded, curriculum-aligned self-reflection practice rather than a one-off post-hoc check. This means framing LLM self-reflection as a sequential learning progression that starts with simple error correction and builds to multi-perspective critical evaluation, mirroring how human students learn reflective thinking over time.
